Implement Actor.call and Actor.reply in C runtime for compiled mode
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/eager-elion","confidence":90,"prompt":"Implement `Actor.call` and `Actor.reply` in the C runtime so they work in compiled mode, not just eval mode.

Context: Currently `actor_call` (synchronous actor messaging with a response) and `actor_reply` only work in the eval interpreter. The compiled LLVM path has no C runtime implementation. This means compiled-mode actors can only use fire-and-forget `send` plus `actor_get_int` to read state.

Steps:

1. Read `lib/eval/eval.ml` and search for `actor_call` and `actor_reply` to understand the eval-mode implementation. Actor.call sends a message to an actor and blocks waiting for a reply. Actor.reply sends a response back from within an actor's message handler.

2. Read `runtime/march_runtime.c` and search for existing actor functions (`march_spawn`, `march_send`, `march_actor_get_int`, etc.) to understand the compiled-mode actor infrastructure. Look at the scheduler, mailbox, and message dispatch.

3. Read `lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml` and search for `actor` to see how actor operations are lowered to LLVM IR / C runtime calls.

4. Implement in `runtime/march_runtime.c`:
   - `march_actor_call(actor_ptr, message, timeout_ms)` — sends a message to the actor, creates a reply channel (could be a mutex+condvar pair or a simple mailbox), blocks until the actor calls reply or timeout expires. Returns the reply value.
   - `march_actor_reply(reply_channel, value)` — sends the reply value back to the caller, signaling the condvar.
   - The reply channel needs to be threaded through: when `actor_call` sends the message, it attaches a reply channel pointer. The actor's dispatch function receives it and passes it to `actor_reply`.

5. Wire up in `lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml` — add LLVM emit cases for `actor_call` and `actor_reply` that call the new C runtime functions.

6. Test with the counter server pattern:
```march
actor Counter(count: Int) do
  on GetCount(reply_to)
    Actor.reply(reply_to, count)
  end
  on Increment()
    { count: count + 1 }
  end
end
```
Verify `Actor.call(counter, GetCount, 5000)` works in compiled mode.

7. Run `dune build` and `dune runtest` to verify no regressions.

8. Update `specs/todos.md` and `specs/progress.md` per CLAUDE.md instructions.

Do NOT use `eval $(opam env)`. Run dune directly.
Read `syntax_reference.md` for March syntax. Use existing .march files as reference."}

nodes.title: Fix cprop corrupting DecRC/IncRC/Free targets
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"claude/dazzling-jang","confidence":95,"prompt":"Fix a bug where constant propagation corrupts DecRC targets in the TIR optimizer. \n\nThe bug: When `match method do \"POST\" -> ...` is inlined and optimized, cprop (constant propagation) folds a variable binding like `m = \"POST\"` into a Perceus-inserted `DecRC(m)`, turning it into `DecRC(literal \"POST\")`. LLVM emit then allocates a FRESH \"POST\" string just to immediately free it, while the original allocation leaks at RC=1."}

nodes.title: Skip subst_atom for EFree/EIncRC/EDecRC/EAtomicIncRC/EAtomicDecRC in cprop.ml
Fix Perceus RC leak for C extern functions (march_string_eq etc)
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/competent-mirzakhani","confidence":90,"prompt":"Fix the Perceus RC leak for C extern functions like `march_string_eq`. The root cause: `borrow.ml` only knows about March-defined functions, so when Perceus processes a call to `march_string_eq` (the `==` operator on strings), it assumes the callee owns its arguments and will DecRC them. But `march_string_eq` only reads — it never calls DecRC. This causes:

1. Spurious IncRC before each `==` call (because Perceus thinks the callee will consume the argument)
2. No DecRC at last use (because Perceus thinks the callee already did it)
3. String literal leaks (every `march_string_lit("GET")` comparison allocates and never frees)

The fix: Add extern borrow annotations to `lib/tir/borrow.ml`

Read these files first:
- `lib/tir/borrow.ml` — the borrow inference pass. Look for how `is_borrowed` works and where it decides if a function parameter is borrowed.
- `lib/tir/perceus.ml` — look for where it calls `is_borrowed` or checks ownership to decide IncRC/DecRC placement.
- `lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml` — search for `march_string_eq`, `march_string_concat`, and other extern C calls to get the full list.

Implementation:
1. In `borrow.ml`, add a hardcoded table of extern C functions and which parameters they borrow. At minimum:
   - `march_string_eq(s1, s2)` — both params borrowed
   - `march_string_concat(s1, s2)` — both params borrowed (it allocates a new string)
   - `march_string_length(s)` — param borrowed
   - `march_string_slice(s, start, len)` — s is borrowed
   - Any other `march_string_*` functions that read but don't own
   - `march_print`, `march_println` — param borrowed

2. Make `is_borrowed` check this extern table when the function is not March-defined.

3. With this fix, Perceus should:
   - NOT insert IncRC before `==` when the argument is borrowed
   - Insert DecRC at the actual last use of the string variable
   - String literals used in comparisons should get DecRC'd after the comparison

4. Run `dune build` and `dune runtest` to verify no regressions.

5. Write a test: a March program with chained string comparisons, run with `--dump-phases`, and verify the generated TIR has proper DecRC placement (no orphan IncRC without matching DecRC).

6. Update specs/todos.md and specs/progress.md."}

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/modest-leavitt","confidence":90,"prompt":"Build five new stdlib modules. Look at existing stdlib modules (like `stdlib/list.march`, `stdlib/map.march`, `stdlib/string.march`, `stdlib/datetime.march`) for style conventions before writing.

## 1. URI module (`stdlib/uri.march`)
URL/URI parsing, encoding/decoding, query string manipulation. Core functions: `parse(string) -> URI` (returns a record/map with scheme, host, port, path, query, fragment), `to_string(uri) -> String`, `encode(string) -> String` (percent-encoding), `decode(string) -> String`, `encode_query(map) -> String`, `decode_query(string) -> Map`, `merge(base, relative) -> URI`. This is needed for every Bastion app.

## 2. Crypto module (`stdlib/crypto.march`)
General-purpose hashing and crypto primitives as a public API. The crypto primitives exist internally for cookies/CSRF but aren't exposed as stdlib. Functions: `sha256(data) -> String`, `sha512(data) -> String`, `hmac(algo, key, data) -> String`, `hash_password(plain) -> String` (bcrypt), `verify_password(plain, hash) -> Bool`, `random_bytes(n) -> Binary`, `random_hex(n) -> String`, `secure_compare(a, b) -> Bool` (constant-time), `base64_encode(data) -> String`, `base64_decode(string) -> Result`, `base64_url_encode(data) -> String`, `base64_url_decode(string) -> Result`.

## 3. UUID module (`stdlib/uuid.march`)
uuid_v4 is already a builtin in eval but there's no UUID module. Build: `v4() -> String`, `parse(string) -> Result(UUID, :invalid)`, `to_string(uuid) -> String`, `nil() -> UUID` (the nil UUID), `v5(namespace, name) -> UUID` (name-based), `version(uuid) -> Int`, `is_valid(string) -> Bool`.

## 4. Duration module (`stdlib/duration.march`)
A Duration type for representing time spans. Bastion cookies, jobs, and workflows all need this. Functions: `new(amount, unit)`, `milliseconds(n)`, `seconds(n)`, `minutes(n)`, `hours(n)`, `days(n)`, `weeks(n)`, `to_milliseconds(duration) -> Int`, `to_seconds(duration) -> Float`, `add(a, b) -> Duration`, `subtract(a, b) -> Duration`, `multiply(duration, factor) -> Duration`, `compare(a, b) -> Ordering`, `format(duration) -> String` (human-readable like "5 minutes, 30 seconds").

## 5. Base64 module (`stdlib/base64.march`)
Standalone Base64 module. Bytes has encode/decode but a dedicated module is standard. Functions: `encode(data) -> String`, `decode(string) -> Result(Binary, :invalid)`, `url_encode(data) -> String` (URL-safe variant), `url_decode(string) -> Result(Binary, :invalid)`, `mime_encode(data) -> String` (with line breaks for MIME)."}

Build comprehensive march-lang SKILL.md for Claude Code sessions
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/wonderful-panini","confidence":90,"prompt":"Build a comprehensive `.claude/skills/march-lang/SKILL.md` file for the March language. This is a skill file that Claude Code sessions will read before working on March code — it needs to eliminate the repeated "exploration phase" where tasks spend 30+ turns reading stdlib files before writing anything.

The skill file should cover:

## 1. Canonical "this is wrong / this is right" pairs
These are the most common bugs generated. For each, show the WRONG version and the RIGHT version side by side:

- `then` instead of `do` in conditionals (`if x then ... end` → `if x do ... end`)
- `fn x do ... end` lambda block form (INVALID — lambdas only use arrow: `fn x -> expr`)
- `fn -> expr` zero-arg lambda (WRONG — must be `fn () -> expr`)
- `pub fn name()` (WRONG — just `fn name()` for public, `pfn name()` for private)
- Leading `|` on type variants (`type Foo = | A | B` → `type Foo = A | B`)
- `module Name do...end` (WRONG — it's `mod Name do...end`)
- `fn _ -> expr` when you want zero-arg (WRONG — `_` is a 1-arg wildcard, use `fn () -> expr`)
- Using `then` keyword anywhere (March has NO `then` keyword)
- `Method` type for HTTP methods (WRONG — `HttpServer.method()` returns `Atom`, use `:get`, `:post` etc.)
- Missing `do` after `if`/`match`/`mod` (these all require `do...end` blocks)
- `fn (x) do ... end` for multi-line lambdas (WRONG — use `fn (x) -> let y = ... \n result`)

## 2. March syntax quick reference
Complete syntax covering: modules, functions, lambdas, types, pattern matching, match expressions, if/else, let bindings, pipes, string interpolation, doc strings, guards, records/maps, lists, tuples, atoms, visibility

## 3. Forge commands
Document all forge commands: `forge new`, `forge build`, `forge test`, `forge run`, `forge deps`, `forge bastion new`, `forge bastion gen schema`, `forge search` (Hoogle-style type search). Show example usage.

## 4. Forge search
Document `forge search` — the Hoogle-style search engine. Show how to search by name, by type signature, by module. Example: `dune exec march -- -search "String -> Int"` or via the search module.

## 5. Stdlib manifest
List ALL stdlib modules with their public function signatures. To build this list:
- Read `bin/main.ml` to get the list of loaded stdlib modules
- For each module, read the .march file and extract the `fn` (public) function names and signatures
- Present as a quick-reference table

## 6. Examples of idiomatic March code
Find 5-10 high-quality example files in the codebase. Look at:
- `test/test_march.ml` for example March code in test strings
- Any files in `examples/` or `bench/` directories
- Well-written stdlib modules like `list.march`, `map.march`, `string.march`
List the best examples with brief descriptions of what they demonstrate.

## 7. Builtins reference
List the builtin functions available in the evaluator (from eval.ml). Group by category: string ops, list ops, math, IO, char ops, map ops, process/actor, HTTP, crypto, etc.

## 8. Pipeline overview
Briefly describe: Parse → Desugar → Typecheck → Eval (interpreter) and Parse → Desugar → Typecheck → TIR Lower → Mono → Defun → Perceus → Borrow → Fusion → LLVM Emit (compiled)

## 9. Testing patterns
How tests work: `dune runtest`, test file structure in `test/test_march.ml`, how to add new tests.

IMPORTANT: Read the actual source files to build accurate content. Don't guess at function signatures — read the stdlib .march files. Read eval.ml for builtins. Read bin/main.ml for the module list. This skill file needs to be ACCURATE because every future task will rely on it.

Save to `.claude/skills/march-lang/SKILL.md` in the repo root."}

nodes.title: Expand stdlib: Enum audit, IO module, System module
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/magical-sutherland","confidence":90,"prompt":"Three stdlib tasks:

## 1. Audit and expand `stdlib/enum.march`
Audit the existing Enum module against Elixir's Enum. Add any missing functions that make sense for March. Specifically check for and add if missing:
- `chunk_every(list, count)` — split list into chunks of N
- `zip(list_a, list_b)` — zip two lists into pairs
- `dedup(list)` — remove consecutive duplicates
- `frequencies(list)` — count occurrences → Map
- `group_by(list, key_fn)` — group elements by key → Map of lists
- `intersperse(list, separator)` — insert element between each pair
- `scan(list, acc, fn)` — like reduce but returns intermediate values
- `flat_map(list, fn)` — map then flatten
- `take_while(list, fn)` / `drop_while(list, fn)`
- `min_by(list, fn)` / `max_by(list, fn)`
- `sort_by(list, fn)`
- `uniq(list)` / `uniq_by(list, fn)`
- `with_index(list)` — pairs each element with its index
- `chunk_by(list, fn)` — chunk when fn return value changes
- `slide(list, n)` — sliding window
- `sum(list)` / `product(list)`

## 2. Build `stdlib/io.march` — IO module
Explicit IO module separate from Process. Functions:
- `read_line()` — read a line from stdin
- `write(string)` — write to stdout (no newline)
- `puts(string)` — write to stdout with newline  
- `inspect(value)` — pretty-print a value and return it (like Elixir's IO.inspect)
- `warn(string)` — write to stderr
- `gets(prompt)` — print prompt, read line
- `iodata_to_string(iodata)` — convert IOList to string

## 3. Build `stdlib/system.march` — System module
System information and environment. Functions:
- `os()` — returns :linux, :macos, :windows
- `arch()` — returns :x86_64, :aarch64, etc.
- `cpu_count()` — number of CPUs
- `monotonic_time()` — monotonic clock (for timing)
- `env(name)` — get environment variable → Option(String)
- `put_env(name, value)` — set environment variable
- `argv()` — command line arguments
- `cwd()` — current working directory
- `pid()` — OS process ID
- `exit(code)` — exit with status code
- `cmd(command, args)` — run external command → Result(String, Int)
- `version()` — March version string"}

Fix default args in TIR/LLVM compiled mode via name mangling
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/cranky-goldwasser","confidence":90,"prompt":"Fix default arguments so they work in compiled mode (TIR/LLVM pipeline), not just the interpreter.

The problem: `lib/desugar/desugar.ml` function `expand_defaults_decl` generates multiple DFn declarations with the same function name at different arities. The interpreter handles this by combining them into VMultiarity (eval.ml:41), but `lib/tir/lower.ml` line ~798 (`lower_fn_def`) expects exactly 1 clause per function and will crash on duplicate names.

The fix approach: Name mangling during desugar. When expanding default args for a function like `greet(name, greeting \\ "Hello")`:
1. Generate `greet$1(name)` that calls `greet$2(name, "Hello")`  
2. Keep `greet$2(name, greeting)` as the full version
3. Generate a dispatcher `greet` that dispatches based on arity to `greet$1` or `greet$2`

This way each generated DFn has a unique name and TIR's lower_fn_def can handle them individually.

Also fix the dune-project version: change `(lang dune 3.6)` to `(lang dune 3.7)` since `depexts` requires 3.7.

After implementing, test with:
1. A March file that uses default args, run through the interpreter (`_build/default/bin/main.exe test.march`)
2. Run `dune runtest` to make sure existing tests pass
3. Update specs/todos.md and specs/progress.md per the CLAUDE.md instructions"}

Fix March parser: allow multi-expression lambda bodies with let bindings
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/optimistic-chandrasekhar","confidence":90,"prompt":"Fix the March parser to allow multi-expression lambda bodies with `let` bindings, just like match arms already support.

Currently this is invalid:
```march
fn () ->
  let x = something()
  let y = x + 1
  y
```

But match arms already support block bodies:
```march
match x do
  Some(v) ->
    let y = v + 1
    let z = y * 2
    z
  None -> 0
end
```

The lambda form should work the same way. The token filter already handles lookahead for match arm block bodies — lambdas need the same treatment.

Steps:
1. Read the current parser (lib/parser/parser.mly) and token filter (lib/lexer/ or wherever the token filter lives) to understand how match arm block bodies work. Look at how `block_body` is defined and used.

2. Read the lexer (lib/lexer/lexer.mll) to understand the token stream.

3. Understand the current lambda parsing — search for `ELam`, `fn`, `arrow`, lambda-related rules in the parser.

4. Modify the parser and/or token filter so that lambda bodies after `->` support the same block_body as match arms — multiple expressions with `let` bindings, where the last expression is the return value.

5. Make sure this works for all lambda forms:
   - `fn x -> block_body`
   - `fn (a, b) -> block_body`
   - `fn () -> block_body`
   - `fn _ -> block_body`

6. Make sure it doesn't break existing single-expression lambdas.

7. Update the AST if needed (lib/ast/ast.ml) — though likely the lambda body is already `expr` which can be `EBlock` or similar.

8. Test: `dune build` must pass. `dune runtest` must pass. Write a test case that exercises multi-expression lambda bodies.

9. Update specs/syntax_reference.md to document that lambda bodies support multiple expressions with let bindings.

10. Update CLAUDE.md's lambda syntax section to reflect the new capability and remove the "no do...end block form" caveat.

Remember: do NOT use `eval $(opam env)`. Run dune/opam directly."}

Improve compiler error messages to be Elm-like
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/strange-goldberg","confidence":90,"prompt":"Chase wants to double down on Elm-like compiler errors in March. Review the current state of compiler error messages and improve them.

Elm-style errors are: friendly, specific, suggest fixes, show the relevant code with underlines/highlights, use plain English (not jargon), and guide the user toward the solution.

Your task:
1. Read through lib/errors/errors.ml and anywhere errors are emitted (typecheck.ml, parser, desugar, eval)
2. Catalog the current error messages — which are good, which are terse/unhelpful?
3. Identify the worst offenders (cryptic messages, missing context, no suggestions)
4. Improve error messages to be more Elm-like: include what went wrong, why, and what to try instead
5. Make sure errors include relevant code spans and context
6. Add "Did you mean...?" suggestions where applicable (typos in function names, wrong argument count, type mismatches with close alternatives)
7. Run the test suite to make sure nothing breaks

Focus on the errors users hit most: type mismatches, undefined variables/functions, arity errors, pattern match issues, and parse errors. These are the first impression of the language."}

User-reported codegen SIGSEGV: ECase var_slot leak (pattern-match shadowing + scrutinee-free)
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/bold-chandrasekhar","confidence":95,"prompt":"The forge codegen bug — explained
The symptom
MutationCompile.compile_expr_m is a 22-arm match on Ast.Expr. When test 37 calls compile_expr_m(ExprParam(1), st), the arm body runs (or its stub does), then on return the function crashes at:

ldr    x0, [x22]          ; x22 holds 0 → null deref
bl     march_decrc_local  ; never reached

[...full bug report...]

The correct fix (in the compiler, not user code)
The epilogue must either (a) be per-arm and only decref slots that arm initialized, or (b) every arm's prologue must null-initialize the union-of-slots so march_decrc_local(null) is a safe no-op."}

Fix unresolved _hash symbol from Map.from_list + user comparator
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"main","confidence":95,"prompt":"Compiler bug: unresolved _hash symbol from Map.from_list + user comparator

march --compile emits an unresolved extern reference to hash (and links fail at the system linker) for any program that constructs a Map via Map.from_list/2 with a user-supplied comparator closure over a concrete key type.

Root cause traced to lower.ml Pass 2 DMod handler not registering module name in _lowered_modules, causing _ensure_module_lowered to re-load the stdlib module from disk without the type_map, producing all-TVar "_" signatures that overwrite the correct ones in fn_table via Hashtbl.replace last-write-wins. Mono then sees only unknown types and cannot specialize hash dispatch.

Fix: register the module name in _lowered_modules in the Pass 2 DMod handler BEFORE calling lower_mod_decls."}

Fix march_decrc crash: closure fn_ptr treated as Bytes heap pointer in Map.fold
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/frosty-einstein-ff988b","confidence":90,"prompt":"Still broken — the two new commits (8287921 ECallPtr / a4909ac) did not touch this path. Identical crash:

frame #0: march_decrc + 60
frame #1: Bytes.list_append$List_Int$List_Int + 212
frame #2: Bytes.concat + 192
frame #3: Encode.encode + 1304
frame #4: Connection.connect_after_tcp

march_decrc is being called with a .text-segment address (a lambda apply-trampoline) — the fault is a write to read-only memory. Same root cause as last round: the triply-curried closure passed to Map.fold inside Encode.encode has a slot whose lifetime/RC treatment ends up mixing the closure's code pointer with a Bytes payload pointer.

The Map.fold call-side crash is gone (commit e1380f7), but the RC ownership side is still wrong — encode(Startup(params)) completes compute, then on scope exit the cleanup walk over the Bytes value decrcs what is actually the closure's function pointer.

Narrowest repro:
fn cat(acc, _k, v) do Bytes.concat(acc, Bytes.from_string(v)) end
test "Map.fold into Bytes accumulator" do
  let m = Map.from_list([("a", "1"), ("b", "2")], str_lt)
  let _ = Map.fold(m, Bytes.empty(), cat)
  assert true
end"}

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"main","confidence":90,"prompt":"In /Users/80197052/code/march/stdlib/http_server.march, the HttpServer.listen function creates an internal pipeline closure:

```march
let pipeline_fn = fn conn -> run_pipeline(conn, plugs)
http_server_listen(port, mc, it, pipeline_fn)
```

`plugs` is captured by the closure. Perceus treats the capture as single-use (last-use move into the closure). When the lambda is called for the first HTTP request, `plugs` is passed to `run_pipeline` which decrcs it to 0 (frees it). On the second request, the lambda loads the now-freed `plugs` pointer → RC underflow.

Reproduction: any compiled sigil/bastion app using HttpServer.plug + HttpServer.listen crashes on the second HTTP request in compiled mode with "march: local RC underflow".

Fix: in http_server.march's listen fn, ensure plugs remains alive across multiple pipeline_fn invocations. Options: incrc plugs before building pipeline_fn (so it has RC=2 when the closure captures it), or restructure run_pipeline to borrow rather than own plugs, or use a workaround like storing plugs in a ref-counted wrapper."}

Fix DCE dropping helper referenced only via defunctionalized Check.all closure
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/lucid-cray-48e736","confidence":90,"prompt":"# Fix: DCE drops a helper still referenced by a defunctionalized Check.all closure (multi-test-file build)

You are fixing a March COMPILER bug (repo: /Users/80197052/code/march). A property test
that typechecks cleanly fails to link, with an undefined LLVM symbol. The compiler is at
commit on branch `claude/fix-whole-program-named-type-placeholders` (or current main);
build the dev compiler with `dune build` and test via the worktree's _build binary — do
NOT reinstall over ~/.opam/march/bin/march.

## Symptom

`forge test` (which compiles all of a project's test/*.march files into one test-runner
binary) aborts at the clang/LLVM stage:

    <project>/test/markdown_test.ll:NNNNN: error: use of undefined value '@all_in'
    1 error generated.
    march: clang failed (exit 1)

`@all_in` is a helper function defined in a DIFFERENT test module (TestProperties) and used
INSIDE a `Check.all(gen, fn x -> ... all_in(...) ...)` property closure. It typechecks fine
(march --check passes); it only fails in native codegen/link. Making the helper `fn`
(public) instead of `pfn` does NOT fix it — so this is not a visibility/export problem.

## Root-cause hypothesis

forge's test runner aggregates every test module's describe/test blocks into one compilation
unit (one .ll). Defunctionalization lifts the `Check.all` lambda `fn x -> all_in(...)` to a
top-level function that emits a call to `@all_in`. DCE (lib/tir/dce.ml) then removes
`all_in`'s DEFINITION from that unit because it is not reachable from the analyzed roots —
but the lifted closure still references it, leaving a dangling `@all_in`. In other words:
the DCE reachability roots do not include symbols referenced from defunctionalized closure
bodies (or the closure-table / function-pointer entries), so a helper that is reachable
ONLY through a closure gets pruned while its reference survives."}

Choose source fix: qualify test-body references in collect_tests via rename_tir_vars — eliminates the name mismatch for the whole pipeline (defun capture, DCE, llvm_emit) instead of patching one consumer

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/peaceful-brahmagupta-981324","confidence":90,"prompt":"March compiler repo: /Users/80197052/code/march. Native codegen miscompiles Bool literal patterns nested inside constructor patterns — the first Bool arm is always taken.

Repro (compile with the dev compiler: `dune build`, then `./_build/default/bin/main.exe --compile -o out file.march`; opam switch `march`, dune at /Users/80197052/.opam/march/bin/dune):

    mod M do
      fn classify(r : Result(Bool, String)) : String do
        match r do
          Ok(true) -> "T"
          Ok(false) -> "F"
          Err(_) -> "E"
        end
      end
      fn main() : Unit do
        println(classify(Ok(true)))
        println(classify(Ok(false)))
        println(classify(Err("x")))
      end
    end

Observed: prints T T E. Expected: T F E. The interpreter (`march file.march` without --compile) prints T F E correctly.

Root cause (verified by reading emitted IR with --emit-llvm): the pattern-matrix compiler (compile_matrix in lib/tir/lower.ml) compiles the nested Bool literal patterns true/false as ADT constructor tests, emitting a heap-style tag switch — `getelementptr i8, ptr %field, i64 8; load i32; switch` — on the Ok field. But Bool fields of ADTs are stored as low-bit-tagged immediates ((n << 1) | 1), not heap pointers, so the tag load is a wild dereference. Because the switch's default branch is `unreachable` and (in observed cases) only one case label survives, LLVM folds the switch and deletes the load — making the FIRST Bool arm unconditionally taken instead of crashing.

Same miscompiled shape exists in stdlib/check.march `format_error` (match r do Ok(false) -> ... Ok(true) -> ...) — it currently prints the right string only by accident of arm order.

Fix direction: when the matrix compiler tests a Bool (or other immediate) literal pattern against a field whose runtime repr is a tagged immediate, emit an integer comparison against the tagged value (or untag then icmp) instead of a constructor-tag switch. Check how top-level (non-nested) Bool matches compile — those work (icmp path) — and route nested occurrences the same way. Watch out: the field's TIR type may be a TVar at the match site even when the source type is concrete (Result(Bool, String)).

Add a regression test in test/test_march.ml: compile the repro through the full pipeline (see emit_actor_ir helper and the `__try_call tags Bool result` end-to-end test for patterns) and assert the output is T F E, or assert at IR level that no `unreachable`-defaulted single-case tag switch is emitted for the Bool field. Run `dune runtest --force` and the benchmarks in specs/benchmarks.md if pattern-compilation hot paths are touched. Update specs/todos.md and specs/progress.md in the same commit. nodes.title: Root cause: Perceus drops a tuple-field binding too early. Pattern 'let t=(a,b,c) in case t of (x,y,g) -> ...use x,y... g()' inserts 'dec_rc g' right after binding g, before g() use → use-after-free. Triggered in practice by default-arg fns inside nested mods: expand_defaults_decl does NOT recurse into DMod, so FPDefault param survives to desugar_decl, making clause non-trivial → params packed into tuple+matched → hits the Perceus bug. Minimal repro /tmp/tup2.march SIGSEGV.

Root cause: native tuple destructure (ECase $TupleN) types fields as TVar _ → ptr+untag, but tuples are built storing Bool/Int raw i64. Mismatch drops Bool true→false. General bug, not default-arg-specific. Repro: any tuple with Bool field destructured.

Fix (b): make tuple ECase destructure use scrutinee's concrete element types (typed/raw), matching construction+EField; leave desugar/typecheck/eval untouched. Rejected (a) [expand nested defaults] — requires absent typecheck source-call resolution.
Add TTuple case to resolve_ctor_fields; use concrete element types for tuple field loads in emit_case
Make expand_defaults_decl recurse into DMod so module-nested default-arg fns expand to arity variants (avoids broken tuple-switch dispatcher)
Fix emit_case: route immediate-literal tag cases (bool/int/atom) off the heap tag-switch path; compare tagged immediates

Pivot: desugar-expand-in-modules fix requires also fixing typecheck qualified base-name->$N resolution (top-level native default calls ALSO fail 'cannot find greet'; nested simple-arg calls work, only closure params crash). Too broad/risky. Better fix: the fundamental Perceus early-drop bug (tup2 repro). Desugar change stashed as backup.

nodes.title: Native lowering for record introspection builtins
nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/upbeat-gauss-9440d3","confidence":90,"prompt":"In the march compiler repo at /Users/80197052/code/march: the seven record introspection builtins — record_keys, record_values, record_entries, record_get, record_put, record_has_key, record_from_list — are interpreter-only (lib/eval/eval.ml) with NO native lowering. Native builds emit bare `call ptr @record_entries(...)` etc. (llvm_emit declares them but nothing defines them), so any project using them fails at link time with "Undefined symbols: _record_entries ...".

Repro: in the depot project at /Users/80197052/code/depot, run `forge test` (the plain native mode). Typecheck is clean; clang link fails on the record_* symbols. The interpreter path (`MARCH_TEST_INTERPRETER=1 forge test`) is fully green — use it as the behavioral oracle. Depot's lib/depot_schema.march, lib/depot_gate.march (cast_record), lib/depot_migration.march, and lib/depot_repo.march all depend on record_entries/record_get/record_put heavily.

Design considerations: records carry no field-name metadata at runtime (fields are stored as a plain sorted-by-name array; see get_record_fields/field_map in lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml), so these cannot be C runtime functions. They need TIR/emit-level specialization per record shape: at an EApp of record_entries with a statically-known TRecord/TCon-record operand type, emit code that builds the List((String, value)) directly. Watch out for heterogeneous field types — the typechecker types record_entries as `a -> List((String, b))` with b unified per call site, so mixed-type records may need the call rejected at compile time with a clear error, or a boxing strategy. record_from_list needs the TARGET record type (from the call's result type) to map keys to slots. record_get/record_put take a runtime key string — emit an if-else chain over the statically-known field names.

Verify with: `MARCH_LIB_PATH=lib:test ~/.opam/march/bin/march --compile --test -o /tmp/dt test/test_aggregates_live.march && /tmp/dt` in the depot repo going green, and ultimately plain `forge test` in depot matching the interpreter run (5275 tests). Add regression fixtures in march's test/ (see test/imports/adt_eq_native/ dune rule pattern for compile-and-run native fixtures)."}

nodes.title: Fix CAS stale compiled-artifact cache after runtime C edits
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"claude/romantic-wilson-b18655","confidence":90,"prompt":"In /Users/80197052/code/march: the compiled-artifact CAS cache returns stale binaries after runtime C sources change. bin/main.ml computes the compilation hash from the TIR module hash + target + opt flags only (see ~line 1071-1090, March_cas.Cas.compilation_hash) — it does not include a hash of the runtime C sources (runtime/*.c) that get compiled/linked into the output binary. After editing runtime/march_extras.c, `march --compile` printed \"compiled ... (cached)\" and served a binary with the old runtime. Also observed: when the cached artifact's cp fails, it still prints \"compiled (cached)\" but writes no output file. Fix: include a digest of the runtime source files (or the runtime .so) in the compilation hash, and check the cp result in the cache-hit path. There is a recent related commit 83ef546 \"fix(cas): invalidate compiled-artifact cache when the compiler changes\" to use as a model."}

nodes.title: Fix hmac_sha256 builtin signature mismatch (typecheck vs eval vs runtime)
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"claude/gracious-murdock-0bae13","confidence":90,"prompt":"In /Users/80197052/code/march: the `hmac_sha256` builtin's typecheck signature (lib/typecheck/typecheck.ml ~line 1342) declares Bytes -> Bytes -> Bytes, but both the eval implementation (lib/eval/eval.ml, returns VCon(\"Ok\",[Bytes])) and the native runtime (runtime/march_extras.c march_hmac_sha256, returns Result(Bytes,String) via make_ok; takes march_string args via string_to_raw, NOT Bytes) disagree with it. stdlib/crypto.march matches `Ok(b)` on the result, which somehow typechecks against the Mono Bytes signature. The llvm_emit return type (lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml ~line 643) says Result(Bytes,String). Decide the canonical signature (likely String|Bytes -> ... -> Result(Bytes, String) to match the implementations), fix the typecheck entry, and add a regression test. Note a separate Bytes-domain variant `hmac_sha256_bytes` (Bytes -> Bytes -> Bytes, bare result) was added in commit d07356d — keep both consistent."}

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/eager-edison-13686a","confidence":90,"prompt":"In the march compiler repo at /Users/80197052/code/march, two pre-existing native-codegen bugs block the depot project (/Users/80197052/code/depot) from passing `forge test` natively. Both are precisely diagnosed; the record introspection builtins that exposed them were implemented in commit cee6f22 (branch claude/upbeat-gauss-9440d3, "feat(codegen,runtime): native lowering for record introspection builtins").

BUG 1 — Tag/untag mismatch across monomorphization boundaries (causes apparent hangs / pointer corruption):
Compiled code stores Ints in generic ADT ctor slots low-bit tagged ((n<<1)|1, see coerce in lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml: i64→ptr tags, ptr→i64 untags with ashr). But when a GENERIC (unspecialized) copy of a function builds an ADT cell from a TVar-typed value (coerce ptr→ptr = no-op, RAW bits) and the CONSUMER is specialized at a scalar type (payload extraction coerces ptr→i64 = ashr), the value gets halved. Concrete repro: depot's stdlib-style alist_get_opt returns Some(v) built in a generic copy with a raw string pointer payload (the opts alist unified at List((String, Bool)) by the typechecker), and the consumer put_*_default extracts d : Bool → ashr → ptr>>1 → garbage pointer (verified: corrupted value 0xa5782218 == 0x14AF04430>>1). The garbage then reaches march_string_eq which memcmp's a bogus huge length — looks like a hang in depot's test "blank uses explicit defaults from field options". Standalone repro: write a main that imports Depot.Schema (MARCH_LIB_PATH=/Users/80197052/code/depot/lib), calls Depot.Schema.define("users", { fields = { role = ("String", { default = "user" }), age = ("Int", { default = 0 }) } }), then Depot.Schema.blank(s), then asserts b.role == "user" — compile with --compile and run; it hangs/crashes while the interpreter path works.

BUG 2 — Generic to_string on type-erased strings (causes wrong-value failures):
to_string(x) where x : TVar emits march_value_to_string (runtime/march_runtime.c ~line 2758), which cannot recognize march_string cells ({rc; len; data} — no tag field) and prints "#<tag:N>" where N is the string's length word. Depot's Depot.Schema.parse_field_spec does to_string(typ) on a type-erased string ("Int"/"String"), so put_typed_default dispatches to the wrong branch and blank() fills wrong defaults (the F failures in depot's test_depot_schema native run).

Verification targets: cd /Users/80197052/code/depot && MARCH_LIB_PATH=lib <march> --compile --test -o /tmp/dts test/test_depot_schema.march && /tmp/dts should go fully green (currently 3 F + hang at "blank uses explicit defaults"); the interpreter run is the oracle. Ultimately plain `forge test` in depot matching the interpreter run. The march repo's own suite (dune runtest) must stay green; note the 2 pre-existing entry_imports_ill_typed rule failures are unrelated.

Design hints: for Bug 1, the principled fix is making the generic/specialized convention consistent — either (a) mono ensures TVar-typed values entering ADT slots from generic copies are tagged exactly when scalars (hard — provenance unknown), (b) uniform tagging at TUPLE slots as well so type-erased bits are always-tagged for scalars (sweeping change: audit ETuple emission, tuple destructuring loads, C code building tuples in march_http.c/march_extras.c), or (c) prevent the unifier from instantiating the same alist payload type at Bool while storing strings in it (the depot code only works because the interpreter is dynamically typed — consider whether the typechecker should reject or the codegen should treat such unifications as fully generic). Look at deciduous nodes 1070-1104 on branch claude/upbeat-gauss-9440d3 for the full decision history, and specs/todos.md "Native lowering for the 7 record introspection builtins" entry for context."}

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/eager-williams-af8b7d","confidence":90,"prompt":"In the march compiler repo (/Users/80197052/code/march): whole-program multi-module typechecking (MARCH_LIB_PATH auto-discovery) accepts ill-typed code that the single-file typechecker rejects, letting type-incorrect TIR reach codegen where it miscompiles (undefined pattern-binder symbols at link time).

Concrete repro:
1. `cd /Users/80197052/code/depot && MARCH_LIB_PATH=lib /Users/80197052/code/march/_build/default/bin/main.exe lib/transaction.march` → correctly errors: "I expected `Result(n2, String)` but found `Bool`" at line 114 (Transaction.run violates `__try_call`'s Bool-only contract — `__try_call : (Bool -> Bool) -> Result(Bool, String)` per lib/typecheck/typecheck.ml:1007, but the callback returns a Result and the unwrapped value is then matched against Ok/Err).
2. `cd /Users/80197052/code/depot && MARCH_LIB_PATH=lib:test /Users/80197052/code/march/_build/default/bin/main.exe --compile --test -o /tmp/t test/test_depot_schema.march` → NO type error (only warnings), then clang fails with `use of undefined value '@$f27180'` — the Ok(v) pattern binder in Transaction.run's `match outcome` is never bound because the i64-typed scrutinee took emit_case's integer-switch path (lib/tir/llvm_emit.ml emit_case), which doesn't bind br_vars, and the unbound var fell into the 0-arg-global-call fallback in emit_atom.

Investigate why whole-program inference (bin/main.ml around line 911, March_typecheck.Typecheck.check_module on the combined module set) doesn't produce the same error the standalone check does — likely constraint-ordering/generalization differences in multi-module mode. Fix so multi-module mode reports the same diagnostics. A defensive secondary fix: emit_case with ADT ctor branches on a non-ptr scrutinee should not silently emit an integer switch with unbound br_vars — at minimum fail with a diagnostic instead of producing an undefined symbol."}

nodes.title: Fix early-CAS hash gap: include source-dir sibling .march files so editing an imported sibling invalidates cached --check/--compile results
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"claude/amazing-merkle-1926c0","confidence":90,"prompt":"In /Users/80197052/code/march, bin/main.ml `compile` builds an early CAS source hash (search for \"Early source-hash CAS\", ~line 745-775): it hashes the entry file source, the stdlib hash, and all .march files under MARCH_LIB_PATH directories — but NOT the sibling .march files in the entry file's own source directory, which lib/resolver/resolver.ml resolve_imports auto-discovers and loads as user code (search_path = source_dir :: lib paths). Consequence: run `march --check entry.march` (or --compile) while all imported sibling modules are well-typed → an OK artifact is stored; then edit a sibling module to introduce a type error; re-run → src_hash is unchanged, the cached artifact hits, and the compiler exits 0 without typechecking the now-ill-typed import. Repro sketch: in a fresh dir, create entry.march that does `use Helper` and helper.march with a well-typed fn; --check (exit 0, caches); add a type error like calling a 2-arg fn with 3 args inside helper.march; --check again — observe exit 0 instead of 1; rm -rf .march/cas and it correctly exits 1. Fix: include the source-dir .march siblings (recursively, same collect_lib_files walk used for MARCH_LIB_PATH dirs, sorted for determinism) in the hash buffer, taking care to not double-add the entry file itself. Add a regression test (dune rule or shell-based) covering the edit-after-cache scenario. Build with dune; dune/opam are at /Users/80197052/.opam/march/bin (not in default PATH)."}

nodes.metadata_json:
{"branch":"claude/wonderful-keller-c592ea","confidence":90,"prompt":"Fix the three P0 items from specs/analysis/2026-07-01-pipeline-deep-review.md (§2, items 1-3), in this order:

1. test/test_properties.ml:730 — the differential oracle skips compiled crashes (`if rc_run <> 0 then None`). Change it to treat a signal-killed compiled binary (exit >= 128 / WIFSIGNALED) as a FAILURE when the interpreter succeeded; keep skipping only clean nonzero error exits.

2. lib/tir/perceus.ml:505-561 (EApp) and :617-677 (ECallPtr extern) — when one variable is passed at BOTH an owned and a borrowed argument position of the same call and is dead afterward, `find_inc_vars` (only inspects non_borrowed_args, :527) computes 0 dups while `post_dec_vars` (:535-561) adds a post-call dec for the borrowed occurrence → double-consumption of one reference → use-after-free. Repro: `both(a:own, b:borrow, n)` called as `both(s, s, 1)` with s dead-after; compiled binary aborts exit 134, interpreter fine. Fix: when a var occupies >=1 owned position in the node, suppress its borrowed-position post-dec (or add one IncRC per coexisting borrowed-dead occurrence). Fix both duplicated sites.

3. lib/tir/perceus.ml:282-285 — `same_arity (TCon(_, ts)) nfields = (List.length ts = nfields)` conflates type-parameter count with constructor field count. The dead-binding FBIP path (:1450-1482) feeds the binding's declared type directly, so a dead `Ok(7) : Result(Int,String)` (1 field, 2 type params) cell gets reused for a 2-field constructor — 8-byte heap overflow. Fix: compare against the constructor's real field count (as add_scrutinee_free_for at :939-944 does), or refuse reuse when the constructor arity can't be verified. Also audit Borrow.has_matching_alloc (lib/tir/borrow.ml:182-199) for the same heuristic.

Add compiled regression tests for both RC bugs (assert interpreter/compiled output parity AND exit code 0). Run scripts/run-tests.sh (full suite) plus the RC-sensitive benchmarks (bench/tree_transform.march, bench/list_ops.march, bench/binary_trees.march) per specs/benchmarks.md. Use MARCH_DEBUG_PERCEUS=1 / MARCH_SANITIZE=1 to verify (see the compiler-rc skill). Update specs/todos.md and specs/progress.md in the same commit."}

nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"claude/wizardly-snyder-bbf58c","confidence":90,"prompt":"In /Users/80197052/code/march, bin/main.ml computes a CAS compilation hash with a `cas_flags` string (two sites, around lines 1160 and 1813; see also the MARCH_SANITIZE usage at lines ~470 and ~2015). The MARCH_SANITIZE env var changes codegen (adds -fsanitize=address,undefined to clang) but is NOT part of the cache key, so `MARCH_SANITIZE=1 main.exe --compile ...` silently reuses a cached non-sanitized binary (observed: second compile printed \"(cached)\" and produced a binary without ASAN). Fix: include MARCH_SANITIZE (and audit MARCH_DEBUG_RUNTIME or any other env vars that alter emitted artifacts) in cas_flags at both sites. Per project memory (project_cas_cache_key_flags.md), verify with a value-revealing program: compile without sanitize, then with MARCH_SANITIZE=1, and confirm the second binary links ASAN (e.g. `otool -L bin | grep asan`). Run scripts/run-tests.sh -q after."}
